title: Multi-Robot Task Allocation Framework
desc: Disaster-response coordination framework for multi-agent robots using cvxpy optimization and ROS. 70% efficiency improvement; validated on TACC supercomputing cluster.

detail.body:
Summer 2024 · TACC · Cyberinfrastructure Research 4 Social Change · Robotics

View the GitHub and documentation for this project: https://github.com/jackpham-rgb/area-disaster-response

[SECTION: What this is]

This project focused on helping multiple robots divide tasks more efficiently. The goal was to improve coordination for search-and-rescue style scenarios, where robots need to cover large areas without wasting time or repeating the same work.

[SECTION: Why I built this]

I wanted to understand how robot teams can work better together. More robots should help, but only if the tasks are assigned well.

This project looked at task allocation using optimization, reinforcement learning, and fairness, so the system could be faster while also keeping work balanced across robots.

[SECTION: What I learned]

I learned that multi-robot systems are not just about movement. They are also about decision-making, coordination, and fairness.

A good system should not only finish quickly. It should also avoid overloading one robot while others do less.

[SECTION: How it works]

First, the system defines the robots, tasks, and environment.

[IMAGE: assets/img/multi-robot-system-overview.svg | Overview of robots, task locations, and the coordination pipeline]

Then the task allocation problem is solved using cvxpy. The goal is to assign tasks in a way that reduces travel cost, overlap, and unfair workload distribution.

[IMAGE: assets/img/task-allocation-optimization.png | Example of optimized task assignment across multiple robots]

After that, the assignments are passed into ROS, where the robots can follow their assigned tasks.

[IMAGE: assets/img/ros-task-pipeline.svg | ROS pipeline connecting task allocation to robot control]

The system is simulated so different scenarios can be tested before using real robots.

[IMAGE: assets/img/robot-simulation.png | Multi-robot simulation]

[SECTION: Results]

The framework improved task allocation efficiency by about 70% and reduced planning time by about 45% compared to baseline methods.

[SECTION: Conclusion]

This project helped me understand how coordination works at scale. It is not just about making one robot perform well, but making a system of robots work together efficiently.

These ideas can be used in other areas like distributed systems, swarm robotics, logistics, and large-scale scheduling. It also connects to future work in embedded and real-world robotics where decisions need to be made quickly with limited resources.
detail.status: Completed. Presented at UT Austin, SCCUR, and STARS 2024.
